TextENGNew: "...Yes, from the banquet side — toward the rear! One more speech to go before the toast. Are you ready back there?!"
"Rear is ready. We can't hear anything inside, so please give us the signal when the speech ends!"
"Listen up, ten beers per table! Make sure you deliver them smoothly to your assigned areas!"
Young hotel staff in neat bow ties were pulling bottle after bottle from beer crates, making ready for the toast.
Dozens of white tables draped in pure white tablecloths filled the enormous hall.
With nearly eight guests seated at each table, some hundreds of people were packed into the space.
The atmosphere was fervent.
Plates of every colour, covered in cling film, were laid out across the tables.
Sushi boats, sashimi, beautifully arranged fruit platters.
Along the walls, chefs had laid out their cutting boards and stood ready to serve fresh sashimi.
The guests seated at those tables were all well-dressed elderly gentlemen whose bearing made it plain they were men of distinction in their respective fields.
And the great, beautiful chandelier hanging from the ceiling silently declared that this hotel was entirely worthy of receiving such guests.
On the dais, a dignified old gentleman was delivering what was, for a pre-toast speech, a rather excessively long oration.
Normally, this kind of long-winded address is deeply unpopular.
Especially just before a toast.
Yet the guests were responding with cries of agreement and bursts of applause, the mood running hot.
Hot may be too generous a word.
Feverish — even strange in its fervour — would probably be closer to the truth.
"It will soon be thirty-eight years since the end of the war."

"In other words, the young upstarts — forgive me, the young people — who grew up without ever experiencing war firsthand are beginning to occupy important positions in companies and government offices alike."
"And yet, for precisely that reason, they are raising their own sons and daughters with the idea that a peaceful era is simply the natural order of things!"
"This is a grave mistake! The freedom and peace that our forebears won at the cost of their blood — how precious it truly is, and how difficult to preserve — the young do not understand this!!"
A roar of agreement erupted from the hall in response to the old gentleman's rallying cry.
changeReason: New translation provides substantially different content with different details and meaning.

TextENGNew: "An era of cultivating individual talent rather than serving the nation? Nonsense!"
"Listen — the reason they can study today, freely choose their dreams and paths, is because their forebears cleared the ground that made it possible!"
"To forget that and live however one pleases is not freedom — it is mere disorder!"
"The number of young people who confuse this, and go on loudly calling for liberation from control and oppression, grows year by year, and grows ever more extreme. It is intolerable!"
"All rights can only be claimed by those who fulfil the duties and responsibilities entrusted to us by the forebears who made those rights possible!"
"That means a firm effort and resolve to preserve a free and peaceful society!"

"In other words — to understand what must be done for the people who will one day live in this Japan, and what is expected of oneself, and to seek guidance from those who came before and carry their will forward!"
"And we, for our part, must not neglect the effort to instruct and guide the young — even if they call us stubborn old men! Therefore we must not yet retire from active life!"

"It is a historical fact that Tokugawa Iemitsu was able to rule as a born shogun precisely because men like Okubo Hikozaemon and Date Masamune served the old Edo shogunate as trusted advisors — and we must prove that truth here and now!!"
changeReason: New translation uses different key action verbs, changing the described events or emotions.

TextENGNew: A thunderous wave of applause broke over the hall.
The elderly gentlemen affirmed it to one another with relish, each signalling that he held the same conviction.
Amid that fever, only two men at the wall remained cool — and in this room, they stood out.
Both wore their suits crisply. Too seasoned to be called young men, but young enough to look like greenhorns beside the elders filling the hall.
"...Why is it that politicians always have to be so preachy — so dismissive of youth? ...Why do they always end up so one-sided...?"
"Simple enough. What politicians want most is votes. Votes come from voters."
"But not everyone over twenty actually votes. Turnout is dominated by retired people — those with time on their hands and, accordingly, a greater interest in politics."
"...So the key to an election is how to win over the elderly demographic."
"Every older person harbours some dissatisfaction with the young. Those who actually experienced the war feel it especially strongly."
"Having lived through desperate hardship themselves, it is only natural that they see the young of today as idle."
changeReason: New translation provides substantially different content with different details and meaning.

TextENGNew: "My mother always talks about the air raids. She used to live near Suidobashi, and she lost her whole family in the bombing — she talks about wandering alone through the burnt-out ruins."
"That's right. Our parents' generation all lived through that war. They faced death over and over, and endured hardships beyond words."
"Which is why they know, in their bones, what peace truly means — and how difficult it is to protect."
changeReason: New translation provides substantially different content with different details and meaning.

TextENGNew: "That's true. For our generation, electricity, gas, water — all of it was simply there. But those things were dug out of nothing and built up by the people now called the elderly."
"And so, without championing their pride and sense of purpose, there is no way to win their votes. 'It is thanks to you that this era is at peace.'"
"'But we must teach the young this too. That is why I am entering politics — to listen carefully to what you intend, and to vote for someone who truly understands you.'"
changeReason: New translation employs substantially different phrasing that alters the nuance or specific details.

TextENGNew: "...I see. Even politicians have quite a difficult time of it getting votes."
"If that conviction is sincere, it stands as a genuine opinion worth respecting. But there are contemptible politicians who only perform agreement for appearances, purely to capture those votes."
"As long as those sycophantic 'political operators' never disappear, nostalgia will never end."

"And so the gap between the consciousness of the young and the old will probably never close — however much knowledge and experience a person accumulates, those in power will continue to refuse to yield it, insisting they are still too immature."
"When will anyone ever be free from that cycle?"
"Simple. Young people should actually go and vote. And instead of being fooled by easy crowd-pleasing, vote for someone with a real policy — an achievable one, mind you."
"Ah, they're out there, aren't they. Candidates running on clearly impossible platforms, hahahaha. Ah — Akasaka-san, isn't that Table F over there?"
"That must be the lady. ...Let's go."
The speech from the dais ended, and the elderly gentlemen all rose to their feet in applause.
Through the middle of it all, the two men picked their way toward an old woman in a kimono.
"Yes? That's me — but who are you, if I may ask?"
"...We arranged to call you yesterday. My name is Akasaka."
"...Ugh... ugh...!! Uuuu...! Hic!! My husband has worked himself to the bone for this country, for so, so long...!! And yet... and yet...!"
"I understand the weight of what you feel, ma'am."
...It was a story one heard all too often.

TextENGNew: "I understand. You have my oath."
Enormous sums of public money flowing from Kasumigaseki.
They were disbursed to subordinate organisations in the name of state policy.
But all manner of ulterior motives crept into the flow, and there was never any shortage of those who inserted a straw into those funds and drew from them like mosquitoes.
Steering benefits toward organisations that served their interests, lavish protections, excessive payouts.
...Business figures drawing close to politicians and officials in order to extract those benefits. Collusion.
Bureaucrats who took shameless advantage of all of it.
This was the structure of Japan's corruption.
Any number of corrupt pipelines bypassed Kasumigaseki, constantly leaking public money.
Finding those pipes and closing them off should, in theory, serve the national interest.
...But it could never simply end with a satisfied nod that wasteful spending had been reduced.
changeReason: New translation provides substantially different content with different details and meaning.

TextENGNew: It would inevitably trigger a pursuit of everyone connected to those pipes, and bring great political upheaval in its wake.

The political world's deepest reason for not pursuing such corrupt spending was not only to protect vested interests — it was also, not infrequently, an awareness of the political damage that would cascade outward.
To put it plainly: the government could not endure the pain of lancing the wound.
The closer the exposed lesion was to the centre, the greater the pain inflicted on the administration, and the more directly it would register in cabinet approval ratings.
...The closer the lesion to the heart, the more catastrophic the damage.
The lesion Akasaka and his team had been secretly investigating was perilously close to the heart — close enough that the administration itself could go into shock.

Intelligence had come in that an enormous outflow of public funds was being channelled through a certain alumni association made up of graduates from seven imperial universities, with numerous prominent figures from various fields counted among its membership. Akasaka's team had been conducting covert surveillance for a long time.

The enemy's solidarity had been firm, but intelligence was received that the wife of one of the organisation's elders — who had been made a scapegoat — felt deep resentment over what had been done, and contact had been successfully established.
They had succeeded, splendidly, in obtaining information of the first importance.
"Good work, everyone! The outer moat is filling in nicely. The material Akasaka-kun obtained in particular is quite something. Must have been hard going — well done."
